title: Panorra API Go New Project Report

issues.display_title: Issue #359: [Redis] Replace Blocking Keys() with Non-Blocking SCAN Cursor
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Implementation: A new private helper function scanKeys(pattern string) ([]string, error) was introduced in Redis.go. This helper uses the Redis SCAN cursor to fetch keys in batches of 100, which prevents blocking the Redis server.
  • Dead Code Removed: During implementation, it was discovered that FindKeysByPrefix() and DeleteKeys() were never used anywhere in the codebase. Both functions were completely removed.
  • Affected Functions: DeleteAllPanoramaStatus and RollupAPIHits now use scanKeys.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🔴 [Redis] Replace Blocking Keys() with Non-Blocking SCAN Cursor

Priority: High
Label: performance, redis, bug
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go

Problem

The current implementation uses redis.Keys(ctx, pattern) which is a blocking command. When executed, Redis halts all other operations and performs a full keyspace scan until completion. This can cause:

  • Server freeze / latency spikes across all users
  • Degraded response time proportional to keyspace size
  • Risk of Redis timeouts on high-traffic environments

Affected Functions

Function Line Pattern Used
FindKeysByPrefix() L40 prefix + "*"
DeleteAllPanoramaStatus() L505 prefix + "_panorama_status:SID:*"
RollupAPIHits() L552 prefix + "_api_hits:*:bucket"

Solution

Replace all Keys() calls with iterative SCAN cursor (non-blocking):

// BEFORE (dangerous — blocks Redis):
keys, err := RedisClient.Keys(ctx, pattern).Result()

// AFTER (safe — non-blocking, iterative):
var keys []string
var cursor uint64
for {
    var batch []string
    var err error
    batch, cursor, err = RedisClient.Scan(ctx, cursor, pattern, 100).Result()
    if err != nil {
        break
    }
    keys = append(keys, batch...)
    if cursor == 0 {
        break
    }
}

Optionally extract into a helper:

func scanKeys(ctx context.Context, pattern string) ([]string, error) {
    var keys []string
    var cursor uint64
    for {
        batch, next, err := RedisClient.Scan(ctx, cursor, pattern, 100).Result()
        if err != nil {
            return nil, err
        }
        keys = append(keys, batch...)
        cursor = next
        if cursor == 0 {
            break
        }
    }
    return keys, nil
}

Acceptance Criteria

  • FindKeysByPrefix() uses SCAN instead of Keys()
  • DeleteAllPanoramaStatus() uses SCAN instead of Keys()
  • RollupAPIHits() uses SCAN instead of Keys()
  • A shared scanKeys() helper is extracted (optional but recommended)
  • No regression in existing functionality

issues.display_title: Issue #360: [Redis] Add Proper TTL to Keys That Never Expire (TTL = 0)
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Initial Implementation: TTLs (ranging from 30 minutes to 24 hours) were initially added to keys that previously had TTL = 0 (never expire).
  • The Rollback: During code review, it was discovered that many caller functions (such as AddBookmark.go, LikeComment.go, etc.) directly fetch security-critical keys like _blocked_post_ec_ and _blocked_users_ec_ via redis.Get(). If these keys expire, the codebase does not have a cache-miss handler to re-trigger the fetch logic (e.g., ReplaceBlockedPostEC()).
  • Security Impact: Expiring these keys would cause the API to assume the user has no blocked posts or users, bypassing the security/privacy blocks.
  • Current State: The TTLs for all these keys were reverted back to 0 (never expire) to maintain legacy compatibility.
  • Future Developer Action Required: Before TTLs can safely be applied to these Redis keys, all ~15 endpoint callers must be refactored to check if the Redis response is empty, and if so, trigger the appropriate replacement function from the DB. Until then, these keys must remain TTL = 0.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🔴 [Redis] Add Proper TTL to Keys That Never Expire (TTL = 0)

Priority: High
Label: performance, redis, memory-leak
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go

Problem

Several Redis keys are set with TTL = 0, meaning they never expire. Over time, as users and sessions grow, these keys will accumulate indefinitely and cause:

  • Uncontrolled Redis memory growth (memory leak)
  • Stale/outdated data being served if not manually invalidated
  • Risk of Redis OOM (Out of Memory) in production

Affected Keys

Function Line Key Pattern Current TTL
SuggestionUser() L98 prefix_list_followings_<encrypt> 0 (forever)
ReplaceDeactiveUser() L258 prefix_deactive_users_ec 0 (forever)
ReplaceBlockedPostEC() L299 prefix_blocked_post_ec_<encrypt> 0 (forever)
ReplaceEmbedPost() L331 prefix_posts_<type> 0 (forever)
ListBlockUser() L381 prefix_blocked_users_ec_<encrypt> 0 (forever)
SetPanoramaStatus() L393 prefix_panorama_status:<sid>:<file> 0 (forever)
IncrementRequeueStatus() L451 prefix_requeue_status:<sid> 0 (forever)
InitializeRequeueStatus() L487 prefix_requeue_status:<sid> 0 (forever)

Solution

Apply appropriate TTL values based on the nature of each key:

// List Followings — refresh every 2 hours (same as suggestion TTL)
redis.Set(ctx, keyFollowing, encoded, 2*time.Hour)

// Deactive Users — refresh every 6 hours
redis.Set(ctx, keyDeactiveUserEc, data, 6*time.Hour)

// Blocked Post per user — refresh every 24 hours
redis.Set(ctx, key, encodedData, 24*time.Hour)

// Embed Posts (villume/tourmkr/etc) — refresh every 6 hours
redis.Set(ctx, key, encodedData, 6*time.Hour)

// Blocked Users per user — refresh every 24 hours
redis.Set(ctx, keyListBlockUser, encodedData, 24*time.Hour)

// Panorama Upload Status — short-lived, cleanup after job done
redis.Set(ctx, key, status, 30*time.Minute)

// Requeue Counter per SID — max session lifetime
redis.Set(ctx, key, 0, 1*time.Hour)

Acceptance Criteria

  • SuggestionUser() — _list_followings_ key has TTL (2h recommended)
  • ReplaceDeactiveUser() — _deactive_users_ec key has TTL (6h recommended)
  • ReplaceBlockedPostEC() — _blocked_post_ec_ key has TTL (24h recommended)
  • ReplaceEmbedPost() — _posts_<type> key has TTL (6h recommended)
  • ListBlockUser() — _blocked_users_ec_ key has TTL (24h recommended)
  • SetPanoramaStatus() — _panorama_status key has TTL (30min recommended)
  • IncrementRequeueStatus() / InitializeRequeueStatus() — _requeue_status key has TTL (1h recommended)
  • No existing key with intended permanent storage is modified

issues.display_title: Issue #361: [Redis] Add Context Timeout to All Redis Operations
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Implementation: A shared helper redisCtx() was added, returning a context with a default timeout of 3 seconds (context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 3 * time.Second)).
  • Rationale: 3 seconds is more than sufficient for standard O(1) Redis operations while protecting the Go prefork workers from hanging indefinitely if Redis is unresponsive.
  • Exception: RollupAPIHits() uses an explicit 30-second context timeout, as it is a heavy background job that performs multiple scans and aggregations.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🟠 [Redis] Add Context Timeout to All Redis Operations

Priority: Medium
Label: reliability, redis, improvement
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go

Problem

Every Redis operation in the file uses context.Background() without any timeout:

redis.Get(context.Background(), key).Result()
redis.Set(context.Background(), key, value, ttl)

This means if Redis is slow, unresponsive, or temporarily down, all operations will hang indefinitely until the default TCP connection timeout (which can be minutes). Since the app uses APP_PREFORK=true, a hanging Redis call will block one of the prefork worker processes.

context.Background() is also repeated 36+ times across the file — a clear DRY violation.

Solution

Introduce a shared redisCtx() helper that returns a context with a reasonable deadline:

// Add this helper at the top of Redis.go
const redisTimeout = 3 * time.Second

func redisCtx() (context.Context, context.CancelFunc) {
    return context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), redisTimeout)
}

Then replace all context.Background() calls:

// BEFORE:
result, err := RedisClient.Get(context.Background(), key).Result()

// AFTER:
ctx, cancel := redisCtx()
defer cancel()
result, err := RedisClient.Get(ctx, key).Result()

Note: For RollupAPIHits() which is a background goroutine, a longer timeout (10–30s) may be more appropriate. Consider passing context from the caller or using a separate helper.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A redisCtx() helper function is added
  • All context.Background() calls in Redis operations are replaced with redisCtx()
  • RollupAPIHits() uses an appropriately longer timeout (e.g., 30s)
  • context import is still present and correctly used
  • No regression in existing functionality

issues.display_title: Issue #362: [Redis] Standardize Redis Client Usage (Global vs Parameter Inconsistency)
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Implementation: The codebase previously had a mix of passing redis *redis.Client as a function parameter and declaring redis := Redis.RedisClient locally (which shadowed the import package).
  • Resolution: All redis *redis.Client parameters were removed from the signatures of 8 functions (e.g., SetPanoramaStatus, IncrementRequeueStatus, etc.). All functions now correctly and directly access the global exported RedisClient.
  • Refactoring Scope: Over 15 caller files across the src/Worker and src/Action directories were updated to match the simplified function signatures.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🟠 [Redis] Standardize Redis Client Usage (Global vs Parameter Inconsistency)

Priority: Medium
Label: refactor, redis, maintainability
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go

Problem

The file has two conflicting patterns for accessing the Redis client:

Pattern A — Local variable shadowing global:

// Used in: SuggestionUser, ListBookmarksUser, ReplaceUserDetails,
//          GetDeactiveUser, ReplaceDeactiveUser, ReplaceBlockedPostEC,
//          ReplaceEmbedPost, ListEmbedPost, ListBlockUser
func ListBlockUser(userId string, encrypt string) []string {
    redis := RedisClient  // shadows the global package-level var
    // ...
    redis.Set(...)
}

Pattern B — Explicit client passed as parameter:

// Used in: SetPanoramaStatus, GetPanoramaStatus, DeletePanoramaStatus,
//          GetRequeueStatus, IncrementRequeueStatus, DeleteRequeueStatus,
//          InitializeRequeueStatus, DeleteAllPanoramaStatus
func SetPanoramaStatus(redis *redis.Client, sid, fileName, status string) {
    // ...
}

Why This Is a Problem

  • Functions in Pattern A are tightly coupled to the global RedisClient — difficult to unit test or mock
  • Pattern B is inconsistent with Pattern A — creates confusion about which style to follow
  • Variable redis := RedisClient shadows the package import name github.com/redis/go-redis/v9, which can cause subtle bugs in the future
  • Callers of Pattern B functions need to pass RedisClient manually every time — boilerplate

Solution

Pick one consistent approach. Recommended: Pattern A (use global RedisClient) for all functions, but rename the local variable to avoid shadowing:

// BEFORE (shadows import):
func ListBlockUser(userId string, encrypt string) []string {
    redis := RedisClient
    redis.Set(...)
}

// AFTER (no shadowing, consistent):
func ListBlockUser(userId string, encrypt string) []string {
    ctx, cancel := redisCtx()
    defer cancel()
    RedisClient.Set(ctx, ...)
}

And update Pattern B functions to remove the client parameter and use RedisClient directly:

// BEFORE:
func SetPanoramaStatus(redis *redis.Client, sid, fileName, status string) { ... }

// AFTER:
func SetPanoramaStatus(sid, fileName, status string) { ... }

Update all callers of Pattern B functions accordingly.

Acceptance Criteria

  • All functions use RedisClient global directly (no local redis := shadowing)
  • Pattern B functions no longer require *redis.Client as a parameter
  • All callers of Pattern B functions are updated to remove the client argument
  • No import name shadowing for the redis package
  • Behavior is unchanged for all affected functions

issues.display_title: Issue #363: [Redis] Remove or Replace fmt.Printf Debug Logs in Production Code
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Initial Implementation: TTLs (ranging from 30 minutes to 24 hours) were initially added to keys that previously had TTL = 0 (never expire).
  • The Rollback: During code review, it was discovered that many caller functions (such as AddBookmark.go, LikeComment.go, etc.) directly fetch security-critical keys like _blocked_post_ec_ and _blocked_users_ec_ via redis.Get(). If these keys expire, the codebase does not have a cache-miss handler to re-trigger the fetch logic (e.g., ReplaceBlockedPostEC()).
  • Security Impact: Expiring these keys would cause the API to assume the user has no blocked posts or users, bypassing the security/privacy blocks.
  • Current State: The TTLs for all these keys were reverted back to 0 (never expire) to maintain legacy compatibility.
  • Future Developer Action Required: Before TTLs can safely be applied to these Redis keys, all ~15 endpoint callers must be refactored to check if the Redis response is empty, and if so, trigger the appropriate replacement function from the DB. Until then, these keys must remain TTL = 0.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🟠 [Redis] Remove or Replace fmt.Printf Debug Logs in Production Code

Priority: Medium
Label: cleanup, redis, logging
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go

Problem

There are 11+ fmt.Printf / fmt.Println debug statements scattered across Redis utility functions. These are problematic in production because:

  1. Race condition in prefork mode — APP_PREFORK=true means multiple worker processes share stdout. Concurrent fmt.Printf calls from different processes can interleave and corrupt log output.
  2. Log noise — High-volume operations (e.g., every requeue increment) will flood stdout with emoji-heavy debug lines, making it hard to find real errors.
  3. Non-structured output — fmt.Printf output cannot be parsed, filtered, or aggregated by log aggregators (Loki, Datadog, etc.).

Affected Lines

Line Statement
L415 fmt.Println("delete Key", key)
L427 fmt.Printf("📊 GETTING: Requeue counter for SID: %s = 0 (not found)\n", sid)
L433 fmt.Printf("📊 GETTING: Requeue counter for SID: %s = %d\n", sid, count)
L436 fmt.Printf("📊 GETTING: Requeue counter for SID: %s = 0 (invalid value)\n", sid)
L452 fmt.Printf("🔄 FIRST ATTEMPT: Creating new requeue counter for SID: %s\n", sid)
L456 fmt.Printf("🔄 INCREMENTING: Requeuing SID: %s, Attempt: %d/100\n", sid, newCounter)
L466 fmt.Printf("🗑️ CLEANING UP: Deleting requeue counter for SID: %s\n", sid)
L480 fmt.Printf("❌ ERROR: Failed to check requeue status existence for SID: %s\n", sid)
L488 fmt.Printf("🔄 INITIALIZING: First attempt for SID: %s (counter set to 0)\n", sid)
L494 fmt.Printf("📊 EXISTS: Requeue counter already exists for SID: %s\n", sid)
L507–523 Multiple panorama status fmt.Printf statements

Solution

Option A (Minimal): Remove all fmt.Printf debug statements entirely. Error cases should return errors, not print to stdout.

Option B (Recommended): Replace with Go's standard log package which is goroutine-safe:

// BEFORE:
fmt.Printf("📊 GETTING: Requeue counter for SID: %s = %d\n", sid, count)

// AFTER (only for genuinely important events):
log.Printf("[Redis] Requeue counter for SID: %s = %d", sid, count)

Option C (Best, future-proof): Adopt a structured logger like zerolog or zap:

log.Info().Str("sid", sid).Int("count", count).Msg("Requeue counter fetched")

For now, Option A or B is acceptable. Verbose per-operation logs (every GET, INCR) should be removed — only keep logs for errors and critical events.

Acceptance Criteria

  • All fmt.Printf and fmt.Println removed from non-error paths
  • Error-level events use log.Printf (or structured logger) instead of fmt
  • fmt import is removed if no longer needed
  • No debug/trace logs remain in production hot paths (e.g., GetRequeueStatus, IncrementRequeueStatus)

issues.display_title: Issue #364: [Redis] Fix Redundant Fallback Logic in IncrementRequeueStatus()
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete

  • Implementation: Removed the redundant Set(key, 1, 0) fallback logic inside IncrementRequeueStatus(). The Redis INCR command automatically initializes a key with 1 if it does not exist, making the fallback on error both unnecessary and potentially harmful (as it would mask genuine Redis connection errors).
  • TTL Note: An initial attempt was made to add a 1-hour TTL when the counter first initialized (newCounter == 1), but this was rolled back alongside Task 02 to maintain legacy cleanup behavior via DeleteRequeueStatus.

Commit result : c3d13c8

🟡 [Redis] Fix Redundant Fallback Logic in IncrementRequeueStatus()

Priority: Low
Label: bug, redis, refactor
File: src/Utils/Redis/Redis.go — L443–L458

Problem

The current implementation of IncrementRequeueStatus() contains redundant and misleading fallback logic:

func IncrementRequeueStatus(redis *redis.Client, sid string) int {
    prefix := os.Getenv("PREFIX")
    key := fmt.Sprintf("%s_requeue_status:%s", prefix, sid)

    newCounter, err := redis.Incr(context.Background(), key).Result()
    if err != nil {
        // ❌ This is redundant: INCR auto-creates the key with value 1
        // if it doesn't exist. This fallback only runs on Redis errors,
        // not on missing keys. Setting key=1 on a Redis error is also pointless.
        redis.Set(context.Background(), key, 1, 0)
        fmt.Printf("🔄 FIRST ATTEMPT: Creating new requeue counter for SID: %s\n", sid)
        return 1
    }

    fmt.Printf("🔄 INCREMENTING: Requeuing SID: %s, Attempt: %d/100\n", sid, newCounter)
    return int(newCounter)
}

Why This Is Wrong

  1. INCR auto-initializes — Redis INCR command automatically creates a key with value 1 if it does not exist. The if err != nil fallback Set(key, 1, 0) is never a correct recovery path — if INCR fails, Set will also fail for the same reason.
  2. Fallback Set has TTL = 0 — Even if the fallback ran, it stores a permanent key.
  3. No TTL on success path — When INCR creates a new key (counter == 1), the key has no expiration, causing memory leak (see Task #2).

Solution

func IncrementRequeueStatus(sid string) int {
    prefix := os.Getenv("PREFIX")
    key := fmt.Sprintf("%s_requeue_status:%s", prefix, sid)

    ctx, cancel := redisCtx()
    defer cancel()

    newCounter, err := RedisClient.Incr(ctx, key).Result()
    if err != nil {
        // Redis is genuinely unavailable — log and return safe default
        log.Printf("[Redis] Failed to increment requeue status for SID: %s, err: %v", sid, err)
        return 0
    }

    // Set TTL only on first creation (when counter transitions from 0 → 1)
    if newCounter == 1 {
        RedisClient.Expire(ctx, key, 1*time.Hour)
    }

    return int(newCounter)
}

Changes Summary

Change Before After
Fallback Set on error Present (redundant) Removed
TTL on key creation 0 (forever) 1 hour
Error handling Silently returns 1 Logs error, returns 0
Client parameter redis *redis.Client param Uses global RedisClient
Debug fmt.Printf Present Removed

Acceptance Criteria

  • Redundant Set(key, 1, 0) fallback is removed from IncrementRequeueStatus()
  • Key receives TTL of 1 hour when first created (counter == 1)
  • Error case returns 0 (safe default) and logs using log.Printf
  • fmt.Printf debug lines are removed
  • Function signature updated to remove redis *redis.Client parameter (align with Task #4)
  • All callers updated accordingly
